claim: The measured chemical composition of the 0Cr steel strip is Mn 20.43 wt.%, Al 9.16 wt.%, C 1.17 wt.%, Cr 0 wt.%, S 0.0006 wt.%, and balance Fe.
evidence_text: 0Cr steel Mn 20.43 Al 9.16 C 1.17 Cr 0 S 0.0006 Fe Bal.

claim: The measured chemical composition of the 3Cr steel strip is Mn 20.04 wt.%, Al 9.09 wt.%, C 1.23 wt.%, Cr 3.09 wt.%, S 0.0009 wt.%, and balance Fe.
evidence_text: 3Cr steel Mn 20.04 Al 9.09 C 1.23 Cr 3.09 S 0.0009 Fe Bal.

claim: The measured chemical composition of the 6Cr steel strip is Mn 20.18 wt.%, Al 9.12 wt.%, C 1.20 wt.%, Cr 6.08 wt.%, S 0.0011 wt.%, and balance Fe.
evidence_text: 6Cr steel Mn 20.18 Al 9.12 C 1.20 Cr 6.08 S 0.0011 Fe Bal.

claim: The Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (x=0, 3, 6 wt.%) steel strips were fabricated by centrifugal casting under near-rapid solidification with a cooling rate of ~1 – 1×10^4 K/s, using a copper mold rotating at 600 rpm, yielding as-cast strips of dimensions 80 mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm.
evidence_text: a near-net shape method was used to prepare lowdensity steel strips which experienced near-rapid solidification with a cooling rate of $1 { - } 1 \times 1 0 ^ { 4 } ~ \mathrm { K } / s$ [30,31]. ... The Fe-20Mn-9Al-1.2C-xCr (in wt. %, $\mathbf { x } = 0 , 3$ and 6, the same as below) steel strips were prepared by a centrifugal casting under near-rapid solidification in an argon atmosphere. ... The as-cast steel strips with size of 80 mm × 60 mm × 2.5 mm were obtained.

claim: As-cast strips were stress relief annealed at 400 °C for 1 h, then cold rolled with a 20% reduction in thickness using multipass rolling on a twin roll mill (Φ160), producing CR20% strips.
evidence_text: Some as-cast steel strips were cold rolled (CR) with 20% reduction after stress relief annealing at 400 °C for 1 h, which was denoted by “CR20%“, hereafter. ... The cold rolling was achieved by multipass rolling and carried out on a twin roll mill (Φ160).

claim: XRD phase characterization was performed with a Rigaku D/Max-2200 using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.154056 nm), scanning from 30° to 100° at 4°/min in continuous mode.
evidence_text: Phase characterization was performed by an X-ray Diffractometer (Rigaku, D/Max-2200, Cu target operated at 40 kV and 40 mA) with a graphite monochromator. The wavelength of the radiation was λ = 0.154056 nm and the scanning angle was $3 0 ^ { \circ } - 1 0 0 ^ { \circ }$ , scanning mode was continuous scanning with a speed of 4°/min.

claim: Tensile tests were carried out at room temperature at a constant strain rate of 2.5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹ on specimens with 4 mm gage width and 12 mm gage length, using an MTS Criterion 44 tester, in accordance with GB/T-228.1-2010, with at least three repeats.
evidence_text: Tensile tests at room temperature were performed at least 3 times by a MTS Criterion 44 Mechanical Property Tester. The tensile specimens with a gage width of 4 mm and a gage length of 12 mm were machined from the steel strips obtained under different conditions. The tensile testing was performed in accordance with Chinese Standard (GB/T-228.1- 2010) at a constant strain rate of $2 . 5 \times 1 0 ^ { - 4 } \ : s ^ { - 1 }$.

claim: The highest product of ultimate tensile strength and total elongation (UTS × TE) of 38.0 GPa% is achieved in the as-cast 6Cr steel strip.
evidence_text: The highest product (38.0 GPa %) of ultimate tensile strength and total elongation (UTS × TE) is achieved in the as-cast 6Cr steel strip.

claim: The ideal stoichiometry of κ-carbide is (Fe, Mn)₃AlC with a perovskite crystal structure; Fe/Mn occupy cornered positions, Al occupies face-centered positions, and C is at the body center (½, ½, ½).
evidence_text: The ideal stoichiometry of the κ-carbide is (Fe, Mn)3AlC, which has a perovskite crystal [5]. In this crystal structure, Fe or Mn atoms occupy the cornered positions, Al atoms occupy the position of the face center, and C is in the center of the cube crystal structure (½, ½, ½) [5].

claim: In the δ-ferrite of the as-cast 0Cr steel strip, the ordered DO3 phase is present with a size of 26 ± 3 nm, and no B2 phase is observed.
evidence_text: the ordered phase in the δ-ferrite is DO3 phase with size of 26 ± 3 nm [36,38,39]. … it is confirmed that the precipitate in the ferrite is mainly DO3 phase.

claim: The volume fraction of δ-ferrite in the CR20% steel strips is 5%, 12%, and 18% for the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr steels, respectively, with no significant change after further annealing at 600 °C.
evidence_text: There is no significant change in the morphology and volume fraction of the δ-ferrite in the steel strips after cold rolling. The volume fraction of δ-ferrite in the cold-rolled steel strips is 5%, 12% and 18%, respectively. … Further annealing at 600 °C has no significant effect on the microstructure of the cold-rolled steel strips.

claim: After cold rolling with 20% reduction, the ultimate tensile strength of the CR20% 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr steel strips is 1154 MPa, 1270 MPa, and 1264 MPa, respectively, and the yield strength also increases with Cr addition.
evidence_text: After cold rolling, the strength of Cr-containing steel strips exceeds that of Cr-free steel strip… the ultimate tensile strength is 1154 MPa, 1270 MPa and 1264 MPa for the CR20% 0Cr, 3Cr and 6Cr steel strips, respectively. The yield strength also increases with Cr addition.

claim: TEM reveals {111} planar slip bands in the austenite of CR20% steel strips, with average slip band spacings of 371±47 nm, 100±15 nm, and 45±13 nm for the 0Cr, 3Cr, and 6Cr steels, respectively; smaller spacing corresponds to stronger reinforcement after cold rolling.
evidence_text: According to these TEM micrographs, {111} planar slip bands are present in the austenite of the CR20% steel strips. … the average spacing of the slip bands in 0Cr, 3Cr and 6Cr steel strips is 371 ± 47 nm, 100 ± 15 nm and 45 ± 13 nm, respectively. The difference in the slip band spacing should be responsible for the difference in reinforcement effect of the steel strips after cold rolling.

claim: Cr addition decreases the size and number of κ-carbides in annealed steel strips by expanding the solid solubility of Al and C in the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, thereby reducing the driving force for κ-carbide formation.
evidence_text: the Cr addition makes the size and number of κ- carbides decrease significantly in the annealed steel strips. The reason should be that addition of Cr results in expanding the solid solubility of Al and C in the Fe-Mn-Al-C steel, which reduces the driving force of the formation of κ-carbide for a given Al and C content.

claim: In as-cast steel strips, the decrease in κ-carbide volume fraction with Cr addition leads to lower yield strength; however, the as-cast 6Cr steel strip has higher yield strength than the as-cast 3Cr steel strip due to solid solution strengthening from higher Cr content.
evidence_text: the volume fraction of the κ-carbides decreases with Cr addition, as shown in Figs. 4–6. Thus, the Cr addition makes the yield strength decrease. … the yield strength of the as-cast 6Cr steel strip is higher than that of the as-cast 3Cr steel strip, due to the solid solution strengthening caused by high Cr content.

claim: Annealing at 600 °C promotes κ-carbide precipitation in CR20% steel strips, with carbides mainly precipitated at dislocation entanglements in the 0Cr and 3Cr steels; however, the yield strength does not significantly increase because high-density slip bands become the main strengthening factor.
evidence_text: the annealing treatment at 600 °C also promotes the precipitation of κ-carbides in the CR20% steel strips. … the κ-carbides are mainly precipitated at the dislocation entanglement in the CR20% + annealed 0Cr and 3Cr steel strips. … Although annealing promotes the precipitation of κ- carbides, the yield strength of the CR20% + annealed steel strips does not significantly increase. This can be attributed to the fact that the high density slip bands become the main strengthening factor for the cold-rolled steel strips.

claim: The higher density of slip bands in 6Cr steel strips results in a higher strain hardening rate because it makes dislocation glide more difficult.
evidence_text: The higher density of slip bands caused by the same strain would result in higher strain hardening rate in the 6Cr steel strips, because it makes the dislocations glide more difficult.

claim: A typical dislocation network structure is present in the austenite of the annealed 0Cr steel strip at a true strain of 0.075.
evidence_text: A typical dislocation network structure is also present in the austenite of the 0Cr steel strip, as shown in Fig. 13 (b).

claim: The slip bands and dislocation arrays in the annealed strips exhibit typical planar slip characteristics related to the interaction between nano‑scale κ‑carbides and dislocations.
evidence_text: The slip bands and the dislocation arrays shown in Fig. 13 (a) and (c) indicate a typical planar slip characteristic [9], which is related to the interaction between nano-scale κ-carbides and dislocations [11,13].

claim: The strain hardening rate of annealed 0Cr steel strip shows a clear double hump feature (Fig. 3d): the first hump is due to slip-band refinement and the second to microband formation. Nano-scale κ-carbides control early strain hardening rate; abundant κ-carbides yield a low early hardening rate similar to microband hardening, creating the double hump, whereas fewer κ-carbides in Cr-containing strips result in higher early hardening rate and a weaker hump.
evidence_text: With respect to the reason for that the strain hardening rate of annealed 0Cr steel strip shows a clear double hump like feature shown in Fig. 3 (d). According to the above analysis, the first hump of the strain hardening rate is related to the slip-band refinement, and the second is due to the formation of microbands. ... When the number of κ-carbides is great, the early strain hardening rate caused by slip bands is low and similar to that caused by microband hardening level. This makes the strain hardening rate of the anneal 0Cr steel strip show a clear double hump like feature as shown in Fig. 3 (d). But when number of κ-carbides is small as the Cr-containing steel strips, the early strain hardening rate caused by slip bands is obviously higher than that caused by microband hardening, which presents a gradually decrease trend in whole tensile process and the hump like feature becomes weaker.
